content:
===BEGIN===
Blockchain achieves decentralized transaction recording through a distributed ledger maintained by a network of nodes, each holding a synchronized copy of the entire transaction history. Instead of relying on a central authority, consensus protocols—such as Proof of Work or Proof of Stake—validate and order transactions. When a user initiates a transaction, it is broadcast to the network where nodes verify its authenticity against cryptographic signatures and the ledger’s current state. Valid transactions are grouped into blocks, which are appended to the chain only after consensus is reached, ensuring immutability and chronological order. The cryptographic linking of blocks via hashes secures the ledger against tampering, as altering any block would require recalculating all subsequent hashes and re-achieving consensus. This architecture distributes trust among participants and eliminates the need for a central bank or intermediary, providing transparency, resilience, and censorship resistance in recording financial exchanges or data transfers.
